title: Apply: plate tectonics
description: Apply your understanding of plate tectonics in this set of free practice questions aligned to NGSS standards.
topic_path: Middle school Earth and space science - NGSS

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which diagram shows the plate movement that forms mountains?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In general, mountains form at *convergent* plate boundaries.
At this type of boundary, tectonic plates move *toward* each other.

So, the correct diagram is

![Two tectonic plates are shown moving toward each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dc0239dd501cdc86f200277e41f6407d.svg)

choices.content: ![Two tectonic plates are shown moving toward each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dc0239dd501cdc86f200277e41f6407d.svg)
![Two tectonic plates are shown moving away from each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7f7d3bed07034c8fd769e65a89bbfce5.svg)
![Two tectonic plates are shown moving past each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d3f29706ebdca3f2ff1b5615f5cb834e.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which diagram shows the plate movement that forms trenches?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In general, trenches form at *convergent* plate boundaries.
At this type of boundary, tectonic plates move *toward* each other.

So, the correct diagram is

![Two tectonic plates are shown moving toward each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dc0239dd501cdc86f200277e41f6407d.svg)

choices.content: ![Two tectonic plates are shown moving toward each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dc0239dd501cdc86f200277e41f6407d.svg)
![Two tectonic plates are shown moving away from each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7f7d3bed07034c8fd769e65a89bbfce5.svg)
![Two tectonic plates are shown moving past each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d3f29706ebdca3f2ff1b5615f5cb834e.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which diagram shows the plate movement that forms rift valleys?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In general, rift valleys form at *divergent* plate boundaries.
At this type of boundary, tectonic plates move *away from* each other.

So, the correct diagram is

![Two tectonic plates are shown moving away from each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7f7d3bed07034c8fd769e65a89bbfce5.svg)

choices.content: ![Two tectonic plates are shown moving away from each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7f7d3bed07034c8fd769e65a89bbfce5.svg)
![Two tectonic plates are shown moving past each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d3f29706ebdca3f2ff1b5615f5cb834e.svg)
![Two tectonic plates are shown moving toward each other.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dc0239dd501cdc86f200277e41f6407d.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows some of Earth’s tectonic plates and their boundaries. The type of boundary between certain plates is indicated using arrows.

[[☃ image 1]]

**At which two plate boundaries is new crust being created?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that new crust is created at *divergent* plate boundaries. At this type of boundary, tectonic plates move *away from* each other.

According to the arrows on the diagram, plates are moving away from each other at two different boundaries. The first is the boundary between the Cocos Plate and the Nazca Plate. The second is the boundary between the Nazca Plate and the Pacific Plate.
So, new crust is being created at the Cocos and Nazca plate boundary and at the Nazca and Pacific plate boundary.

options.explanation: Map modified from [U.S. Geological Survey](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Plates_tect2_en.svg) on Wikimedia Commons, public domain

options.alt: A map of several tectonic plates around North and South America is shown. Arrows indicate that certain pairs of plates are moving toward or away from each other. The Cocos Plate and the Caribbean Plate are moving toward each other. The Cocos Plate and the Nazca Plate are moving away from each other. The Nazca Plate and the Pacific Plate are moving away from each other. The Nazca Plate and the South American Plate are moving toward each other.

choices.content: the Cocos and Caribbean plate boundary
the Cocos and Nazca plate boundary
the Nazca and Pacific plate boundary
the Nazca and South American plate boundary

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows some of Earth’s tectonic plates and their boundaries. The type of boundary between certain plates is indicated using arrows.

[[☃ image 1]]

**At which two plate boundaries is old crust being destroyed?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that old crust is destroyed at *convergent* plate boundaries. At this type of boundary, tectonic plates move *toward* each other.

According to the arrows on the diagram, plates are moving toward each other at two different boundaries. The first is the boundary between the Cocos Plate and the Caribbean Plate. The second is the boundary between the Nazca Plate and the South American Plate.
So, old crust is being destroyed at the Cocos and Caribbean plate boundary and at the Nazca and South American plate boundary.

options.explanation: Map modified from [U.S. Geological Survey](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Plates_tect2_en.svg) on Wikimedia Commons, public domain

options.alt: A map of several tectonic plates around North and South America is shown. Arrows indicate that certain pairs of plates are moving toward or away from each other. The Cocos Plate and the Caribbean Plate are moving toward each other. The Cocos Plate and the Nazca Plate are moving away from each other. The Nazca Plate and the Pacific Plate are moving away from each other. The Nazca Plate and the South American Plate are moving toward each other.

choices.content: the Cocos and Caribbean plate boundary
the Cocos and Nazca plate boundary
the Nazca and Pacific plate boundary
the Nazca and South American plate boundary

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows some of Earth’s tectonic plates and their boundaries. The type of boundary between certain plates is indicated using arrows.

[[☃ image 1]]

**At which plate boundary is old crust being destroyed?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, remember that old crust is destroyed at *convergent* plate boundaries. At this type of boundary, tectonic plates move *toward* each other.
According to the arrows on the diagram, plates are moving toward each other at the boundary between the Antarctic Plate and the South American Plate.
So, old crust is being destroyed at the Antarctic and South American plate boundary.

options.explanation: Map modified from [U.S. Geological Survey](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Plates_tect2_en.svg) on Wikimedia Commons, public domain

options.alt: A map of several tectonic plates around North and South America is shown. Arrows indicate that certain pairs of plates are moving toward, away from, or past each other. The North American Plate and the Pacific Plate are moving past each other. The Cocos Plate and the Nazca Plate are moving away from each other. The Antarctic Plate and the South American Plate are moving toward each other.

choices.content: the North American and Pacific plate boundary
the Cocos and Nazca plate boundary
the Antarctic and South American plate boundary

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the densities of two oceanic plates at a convergent boundary.

Plate | Density $\left(\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}\right)$
:-: | :-:
A | $2.85$
B | $3.20$

**Based on the information in the table, what will most likely happen as the two plates converge?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: When two oceanic plates of different densities meet at a convergent boundary, the more dense plate is pushed below the less dense plate.

Let’s compare the densities of plates A and B. According to the table, plate A has a *lower* density $\left(2.85\,\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}\right)$ than plate B does $\left(3.20\,\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}\right)$.
So, as the two plates converge, plate B will be pushed below plate A.

choices.content: Plate A will be pushed below plate B.
Plate B will be pushed below plate A.
Both plates will be pushed upwards.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the densities of two oceanic plates at a convergent boundary.

Plate | Density $\left(\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}\right)$
:-: | :-:
A | $3.15$
B | $2.75$

**Based on the information in the table, what will most likely happen as the two plates converge?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: When two oceanic plates of different densities meet at a convergent boundary, the more dense plate is pushed below the less dense plate.

Let’s compare the densities of plates A and B. According to the table, plate A has a *higher* density $\left(3.15\,\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}\right)$ than plate B does $\left(2.75\,\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}\right)$.
So, as the two plates converge, plate A will be pushed below plate B.

choices.content: Plate A will be pushed below plate B.
Plate B will be pushed below plate A.
Both plates will be pushed upwards.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the densities of two oceanic plates at a convergent boundary.

Plate | Density $\left(\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}\right)$
:-: | :-:
A | $2.70$
B | $3.05$

**Based on the information in the table, what will most likely happen as the two plates converge?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: When two oceanic plates of different densities meet at a convergent boundary, the more dense plate moves below the less dense plate.

Let’s compare the densities of plates A and B. According to the table, plate A has a *lower* density $\left(2.70\,\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}\right)$ than plate B does $\left(3.05\,\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}\right)$.
So, as the two plates converge, plate B will move below plate A.

choices.content: Plate A will move below plate B.
Plate B will move below plate A.
Both plates will stop moving.

assessment_items.question_content:
The cross section below represents the oceanic crust found on both sides of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge. Samples of crust were collected at points X, Y, and Z shown on the diagram.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which sequence correctly lists the crust samples from youngest to oldest?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The age of oceanic crust depends on its distance from the mid-ocean ridge where it was created. In general, newer crust is found closer to the ridge, while older crust is found farther from the ridge.

Let’s use the diagram to determine the distance of each crust sample from the ridge:

Sample | Distance from ridge $(\text{km})$
- | -
X| between $60$ and $80$
Y | between $0$ and $20$
Z | between $20$ and $40$

Based on this data, we can see that sample Y is closest to the ridge, while sample X is farthest from the ridge.
So, the sequence that correctly lists the crust samples from youngest to oldest is Y $\rightarrow$ Z $\rightarrow$ X.

options.alt: A number line is shown that goes from 80 km on the left to 80 km on the right with tick marks every 20 km. The origin at 0 is labeled Mid-Atlantic Ridge. The line is labeled Distance from ridge (km). There are 3 points marked on the line. Point X is about 66 units to the left of 0. Point Y is about 5 units to the left of 0. Point Z is about 28 units to the right of 0.

choices.content: X $\rightarrow$ Y $\rightarrow$ Z
X $\rightarrow$ Z $\rightarrow$ Y
Y $\rightarrow$ X $\rightarrow$ Z
Y $\rightarrow$ Z $\rightarrow$ X

assessment_items.question_content:
The cross section below represents the oceanic crust found on both sides of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge. Samples of crust were collected at points X, Y, and Z shown on the diagram.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which sequence correctly lists the crust samples from youngest to oldest?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The age of oceanic crust depends on its distance from the mid-ocean ridge where it was created. In general, newer crust is found closer to the ridge, while older crust is found farther from the ridge.

Let’s use the diagram to determine the distance of each crust sample from the ridge:

Sample | Distance from ridge $(\text{km})$
- | -
X| between $20$ and $40$
Y | between $0$ and $20$
Z | between $40$ and $60$

Based on this data, we can see that sample Y is *closest* to the ridge, while sample Z is *farthest* from the ridge.
So, the sequence that correctly lists the crust samples from youngest to oldest is Y $\rightarrow$ X $\rightarrow$ Z.

options.alt: A number line is shown that goes from 80 km on the left to 80 km on the right with tick marks every 20 km. The origin at 0 is labeled Mid-Atlantic Ridge. The line is labeled Distance from ridge (km). There are 3 points marked on the line. Point X is about 26 units to the left of 0. Point Y is about 5 units to the left of 0. Point Z is about 54 units to the right of 0.

choices.content: Y $\rightarrow$ X $\rightarrow$ Z
Y $\rightarrow$ Z $\rightarrow$ X
Z $\rightarrow$ X $\rightarrow$ Y
Z $\rightarrow$ Y $\rightarrow$ X
